title: Writing a Research Proposal - Multiple Choice

questions.question: A group writes their proposal and includes a Results section summarising the expected outcomes of the planned study. Is including a Results section in a proposal RIGHT or WRONG, and why?

options.A: RIGHT — a proposal needs results so the reader can judge whether the study contributed
options.B: RIGHT — listing expected results shows the reader the methods were well designed
options.C: WRONG — a proposal plans a future study, so it has no results and no discussion yet
options.D: WRONG — results belong in the proposal but the discussion section must come first

questions.explanation: A proposal is a plan for a study not yet run, so it contains no results and no discussion. A and B treat the proposal like a paper that reports a finished study; D wrongly accepts a results section into the proposal.

questions.question: What is the single most important job of a research proposal?

options.A: To get the planned study approved by convincing the supervisor to green-light it
options.B: To report the findings of the study to the wider scientific community clearly
options.C: To prove the study already contributed to both science and the real world
options.D: To lock the methods so that nothing about the study can be changed later

questions.explanation: A proposal's only job is to get the study approved by convincing your supervisor to green-light it. B and C describe a paper about an already-conducted study; D is wrong because methods may legitimately change later.

questions.question: A reviewer reads a proposal and can comfortably shrug and say 'so what if we don't know this?'. According to the section, which part of the proposal has most clearly failed?

options.A: The background, because it did not list enough peer-reviewed sources
options.B: The motivation, because it failed the 'so what?' test of relevance
options.C: The methods, because they were not described in replicable detail
options.D: The research question, because it was phrased far too broadly

questions.explanation: The 'so what?' test targets the motivation: if a reader can shrug at your question, the motivation has failed to establish relevance. A, C, and D name other sections, but the shrug-test is explicitly the diagnostic for a weak motivation.

questions.question: Which two kinds of relevance must a proposal's motivation establish?

options.A: Statistical relevance and practical replicability of the chosen design
options.B: Historical relevance and the personal interest of the group members
options.C: Methodological relevance and the novelty of the data set used

questions.explanation: The motivation must establish BOTH scientific relevance AND real-world (practical/societal) relevance. A, B, and C mix in concepts that belong to methods or are simply not the two required kinds of relevance.

questions.question: A student backs the real-world relevance of their motivation only with a vivid personal anecdote about a friend. Is this sufficient support, RIGHT or WRONG, and why?

options.A: RIGHT — an anecdote is the strongest possible evidence for real-world relevance one can give
options.B: RIGHT — anecdotes fully count as scientific sources when they are detailed enough
options.C: WRONG — relevance must be proven, and only the background section may use anecdotes
options.D: WRONG — an anecdote is a hook; back relevance with news or statistics

questions.explanation: An anecdote is a useful hook but not enough; real-world relevance should be backed with news, statistics, or the future-work sections of peer-reviewed papers. A and B overrate the anecdote; C invents a rule about which section may use anecdotes.

questions.question: What is the key test the methods section of a proposal must pass?

options.A: Could someone replicate your study from this description?
options.B: Does the section cite at least five peer-reviewed sources?
options.C: Will the planned analyses produce a statistically significant result?
options.D: Does the section prove the study will save lives in practice?

questions.explanation: The methods must convince the reader the approach is correct and replicable; the key test is whether someone could replicate the study from the description. B is a background requirement, C cannot be promised in advance, and D is an example of overclaiming.

questions.question: According to the section, which source-use error is the MOST common and the hardest skill to fix?

options.A: Lumping all citations together at the very end of a paragraph
options.B: Listing sources one by one instead of synthesising them
options.C: Making claims with no citation at all attached to them
options.D: Citing sources that the writer has never actually read

questions.explanation: Listing instead of synthesising ('Smith said X. Furthermore, Jones said Y.') is the most common and hardest-to-master error. A is the least common, C happens constantly but is mid-frequency, and D is an AI-related verification issue, not one of the three ranked source-use errors.

questions.question: In the dinner-party analogy for writing with sources, what does introducing each guest one-by-one represent?

options.A: A synthesis, because every guest is given a fair turn to speak in order
options.B: A critique, because each guest is judged separately on their merits
options.C: A summary, because it tells what each source says with no overarching story
options.D: A citation, because naming each guest is the same as an in-text reference

questions.explanation: Introducing guests one-by-one is a summary: it reports each source separately with no story. Synthesis (A) instead explains why they were all invited together; B and D confuse the analogy with the 5 C's terms.

questions.question: A group asks an AI tool to find the five scientific sources for their background and pastes its reference list straight in. Is this RIGHT or WRONG, and why?

options.A: RIGHT — tools such as Consensus and Elicit are reliable enough to source papers
options.B: RIGHT — AI may both find and summarise sources as long as they are later cited
options.C: WRONG — AI hallucinates sources, so every citation must trace to a paper you read
options.D: WRONG — AI may help only by writing the full background paragraph for the group

questions.explanation: Finding sources with AI is forbidden because it hallucinates both the source and its contents, even tools like Consensus, Elicit, or Perplexity; every citation must trace to a real paper you read. A and B trust the tools too far; D is forbidden because generating content to submit lets AI invent facts.

questions.question: When reading a completed synthesis matrix, a mostly-empty row appears for one idea. What does the section say this signals?

options.A: The idea is too big and should be split into several smaller ideas
options.B: The matrix was read vertically, which always produces a summary
options.C: The idea is well supported and ready to anchor its own paragraph
options.D: The idea is weakly supported; drop it or find more sources for it

questions.explanation: A mostly-empty row means few sources address that idea, so you should drop it or find more sources. A describes the opposite diagnostic (a very full row), B confuses reading direction with row fullness, and C contradicts the weak support an empty row shows.
